steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student claims that Argentina and the United States produce agricultural goods more cheaply than India and Russia. The passage explains that when tariffs are eliminated, cheaper producers see production increase (consumers prefer their goods) while more expensive producers see production decrease (consumers turn to cheaper imports). We need the table finding that best connects production changes to the student's cost claim.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Although agricultural production in India and Russia would likely decrease if tariffs were eliminated, it would likely increase in both Argentina and the United States.’: This is exactly the pattern the passage predicts for expensive vs. cheap producers — decrease for the expensive ones, increase for the cheap ones. It directly supports the claim. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘Although eliminating tariffs would likely cause agricultural market prices to decrease in most locations, it would likely cause agricultural market prices to increase in both Argentina and the United States.’: Price changes relate to import prices, not to which country is a cheaper producer. This does not logically support the cost-of-production claim. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Argentina and the United States are the only countries shown that are projected to see both an increase in agricultural production and a reduction in agricultural prices in the absence of tariffs.’: Even if true, a reduction in prices alongside a production increase reflects market competition, not necessarily that those countries are cheaper producers than India and Russia. The comparison is indirect. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘In the absence of tariffs, total agricultural production in Argentina and the United States would likely exceed that in India and Russia.’: Comparing absolute output levels says nothing about relative production costs; a large country can produce more simply because of its size. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
